doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectatic changes in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and lung bases. Minimal gravitational changes in the dorsal lungs. Bilateral pleuroapical scarring. A few calcifications in the lungs, largest in the left lower lobe measuring up to 5 mm. Pulmonary nodules in series 3 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measuring up to 5 mm (series 103), perifissural.
A few micronodules measuring up to 2 mm are also seen (left perifissural - series 121). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Cardia of the stomach protrudes into the posterior mediastinum with formation of a hiatal hernia, diastasis of the diaphragmatic crura up to 2.4 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification of the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and at the ostium of the right coronary artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Calcification of the aortic valve.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased vertebral body height at the T4-T10 levels up to 25%. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased bone density in vertebrae at the T11-L2 level up to +74 HU. A few subchondral cysts in the right humeral head measuring up to 8 mm, in the left scapula up to 9 mm.

Upper abdomen: Hypodense lesions in the liver (at least three) with sharp smooth contours, fluid/cystic density, measuring up to 1.9 × 1.6 cm. Two hypodense lesions of similar appearance are seen subcapsularly in the left kidney on this examination, ranging from 5 mm to 1.3 × 1.2 cm. A calculus in the lower calyceal group of the left kidney measures up to 7 × 4 mm, without signs of urinary tract obstruction.
doc_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe, a few micronodules in the lungs: per Fleischner Society recommendations, no follow-up is required in low-risk patients; in high-risk patients, optional CT at 12 months.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

3. Calcification of the aortic valve. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, decreased vertebral body height at T4-T10, decreased vertebral body density at T11-L2. A few subchondral cysts in the bones (Bone-RADS1).

5. Hypodense liver lesions (per "Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee," 2017, no further follow-up is required in the absence of hepatic risk factors).

6. Hypodense lesions in the left kidney (per "Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee," 2018, no further follow-up is required).

7. Left nephrolithiasis without signs of urinary tract obstruction.

8. Hiatal hernia without signs of incarceration.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 3 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures up to 5 mm (image 103), perifissural.
Left lower lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures up to 4 mm (image 120), healed granuloma, requires no follow-up.
A few micronodules measure up to 2 mm in both lungs, left perifissural micronodules (image 121). Multiple linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and lung bases. Minimal gravitational changes in the dorsal lungs. Bilateral pleuroapical scarring. No pleural effusion. Mild bronchial wall thickening. 

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Cardia of the stomach protrudes into the posterior mediastinum with formation of a hiatal hernia.

Heart: The heart is mildly enlarged, cardiothoracic ratio 0.6. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification of the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Interarterial course of the right coronary artery, with moderate calcification. Calcification of the aortic valve.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased vertebral body height at the T4-T10 levels up to 30% (Genant grade 2) and decreased bone density in vertebrae at the T11-L2 level up to +74 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of shoulder joints. 

Upper abdomen: Multiple hypodense lesions in the liver with sharp smooth contours, fluid density, measure up to 1.9 × 1.6 cm. Two hypodense lesions are seen subcapsularly in the left kidney, measuring 1.4 cm and 1.1 cm. A calculus in the lower calyceal group of the left kidney measures up to 7 mm, without signs of urinary tract obstruction.
val_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe measures up to 5 mm, with a few micronodules in the lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications, or intraoperative CTs.

3. Calcification of the aortic valve. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Hypodense liver lesions, likely benign. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

5. Hypodense lesions in the left kidney, likely benign cysts. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: No need for further work-up. Left nephrolithiasis without signs of urinary tract obstruction.

6. Hiatal hernia.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Lung nodule.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 6.72 mGy; total DLP 223.69 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Lung nodules in series 6 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid micronodule measuring 3 mm on image 80.
- Solid micronodule measuring 3 mm on image 88.
- Solid polygonal nodule along the major fissure measuring 3 mm on image 102.
Polymorphous linear opacities and perilobular opacities are present in the lungs, greatest in the right middle and lower lobes, as well as linear atelectatic changes in the posterior basal regions. Focally dilated segmental and subsegmental bronchi are noted in areas of linear opacities.
Areas of paraseptal emphysema are present in the upper lungs.
In the supradiaphragmatic right lung, there is an air cyst with irregular contours and thin internal septa, measuring 1.8 × 1.4 cm.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the spine: osteochondrosis, spondylosis.

Upper abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right hepatic lobe, there is a low-density lesion with indistinct margins, measuring approximately 2.3 × 2.0 cm.
doc_impression:
1. Solid micronodules in the right lower lobe. CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended in accordance with the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations. Subpleural polygonal nodule in the right lower lobe. No follow-up required.

2. CT appearance of polymorphous linear opacities in the lungs, likely consistent with the subpleural fibrotic type of interstitial lung abnormalities (ILA). Given the accompanying pulmonary nodules and the high risk of progression of both processes, interval follow-up is indicated. Follow-up chest CT in 12 months with baseline pulmonary function assessment is recommended. Clinical and imaging progression, including development of respiratory symptoms, warrants earlier follow-up imaging.

3. Mild paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

4. Right hepatic lobe lesion. Per ACR recommendations (2017), further evaluation is required. Liver MRI with intravenous contrast is recommended for differential diagnosis, to exclude a solid neoplasm and further characterize the lesion.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Lung nodules in series 6 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 3 mm on image 80.
- Solid nodule measures 3 mm on image 88.
- Solid perifissural nodule measures 3 mm on image 102.
In the middle and lower lobes of the right lung there are multiple subsegmental linear atelectasis and small round atelectasis, adjacent to the pleura, traction bronchiectasis. In the supradiaphragmatic regions of the lungs on the left in the lower lobe there are several subsegmental atelectasis. Areas of mild paraseptal emphysema are present in the upper lungs. In the supradiaphragmatic right lung, there is an air cyst, measuring 1.8 × 1.4 cm, clinically insignificant. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the spine.

Upper abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right hepatic lobe, there is a low-density lesion with indistinct margins, subdiaphragmatic, measuring approximately 2.3 × 2.0 cm (image 174).
val_impression:
1. Multiple subsegmental atelectasis of the lower and middle lobes of the right lung and the lower lobe of the left lung, possibly due to hypoventilation associated with chest pain and limited chest mobility. No pleural effusion is identified. Follow-up Chest CT is recommended.

2. Subdiaphragmatic right hepatic lobe lesion. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: The current study is inadequate to characterize this lesion. Prompt MRI is recommended for further evaluation.

3. Solid nodules in the right lower lobe up to 3 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

4. Mild paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Pleural effusion. Chest pain for 1 month.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 16.4 mGy; total DLP 523.4 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 3 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 1.6 × 1.1 cm on images 60-70 (increased in size; measured 1.4 × 0.9 cm on the study from August 26, 2025) with pseudocavitation.
Subpleural and peribronchial solid nodules in the right upper, middle, and lower lobes and left upper and lower lobes, measuring up to 3.0 × 1.4 cm, the largest in the right upper lobe, series 3, images 56-71. Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, a new pseudocavity is present within the right lower lobe nodule, likely bronchiectasis; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from August 26, 2025, some nodules in both lungs have decreased in size.
In both lungs, there is irregular increase and distortion of the pulmonary markings, with thickening of the interstitial structures, more pronounced centrally, reticular changes, and areas of consolidation. On this background, lumina of irregularly dilated bronchi are seen; some contain bronchial contents. Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, new bronchial contents are present in one of the largest bronchiectatic airways in the right upper lobe, series 3, image 79; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from August 26, 2025, the areas of consolidation in both lungs have decreased in size. Mosaic attenuation of the lung parenchyma bilaterally. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass. Enlarged prevascular, upper paratracheal (station 2R), upper paratracheal (station 4R), and subcarinal lymph nodes are present, measuring up to 1.4 cm in short axis (increase in size of some lymph nodes compared with CT from December 15, 2025). Hiatal hernia with a portion of the stomach extending into the lower posterior mediastinum, measuring up to 2.7 × 1.8 cm.

Heart: Cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.56). No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending and left circumflex coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated; moderate wall calcification is present. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.8 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen: Cholecystectomy. Left adrenal mass with density of 6 HU, measuring 3.1 × 2.0 cm, with smooth margins (unchanged compared with studies from December 15, 2025 and August 26, 2025).
doc_impression:
1. Areas of consolidation in both lungs, thickening of the interstitial structures, and reticular changes in both lungs may represent interstitial lung disease (nonspecific interstitial pneumonia should be considered first). Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, bronchial contents have appeared in a right upper lobe bronchiectatic airway; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from August 26, 2025, the areas of consolidation in both lungs have decreased in size, which may have been due to superimposed inflammatory changes on the background of the underlying disease.

2. Subpleural and peribronchial solid nodules in the right upper, middle, and lower lobes and left upper and lower lobes. Compared with CT from December 15, 2025, there is an increase in size of the left upper lobe nodule and a new pseudocavity within the right lower lobe nodule, likely bronchiectasis; otherwise unchanged. Compared with CT from August 26, 2025, some nodules in both lungs have decreased in size, with increase in size of the left upper lobe nodule. Mosaic attenuation of the lung parenchyma.

3. Intrathoracic lymphadenopathy, with increase in size of some lymph nodes compared with CT from December 15, 2025.

4. Cardiomegaly. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.8 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

7. Stable left adrenal mass; according to Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, no further follow-up is required.

8. Hiatal hernia.

9. Cholecystectomy.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple peribronchial and subpleural consolidation areas, areas of ground-glass with crazy paving, reticulations, traction bronchiectasis, compared with CT on August 26, 2025, with mild interval worsening in the volume of lung parenchyma involvement. Diffuse mosaic attenuation. In the left upper lobe, solid nodule with pseudocavitation measures 1.6 × 1.1 cm on images 60-70 (increased in size; measured 1.4 × 0.9 cm on Chest CT on August 26, 2025). Multiple consolidation-like nodules measure up to 1.4 cm with pseudocavitation, mostly unchanged. Compared to CT on December 15, 2025, a new pseudocavity is present within the right lower lobe nodule (image 96), likely bronchiectasis. Compared to CT on December 15, 2025, the pseudocavity in image 70 is filled with content now (image 78). No honeycombing. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass. Enlarged prevascular, upper paratracheal (station 2R), upper paratracheal (station 4R), and subcarinal lymph nodes are present, measuring up to 1.4 cm in short axis (increase in size of some lymph nodes compared to CT on December 15, 2025). Hiatal hernia with stomach cardia.

Heart: Cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.6). No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcifications are present in the left anterior descending and left circumflex coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcifications. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.6 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen: Cholecystectomy. Left adrenal mass with density of 6 HU, measures 2.0 cm, with smooth margins (unchanged compared to December 15, 2025 and August 26, 2025).
val_impression:
1. Diffuse interstitial lung disease with a slight interval worsening compared to Chest CT on December 15, 2025, August 26, 2025. Given the presence of consolidation and nodules with subpleural and peribronchial distribution, ground-glass opacities, crazy paving, cryptogenic organizing pneumonia or other interstitial lung diseases are possible. Subpleural and peribronchial solid nodules in both lungs likely represent diffuse lung disease. In the right upper lobe, solid nodule with pseudocavitation increased in size compared to Chest CT on August 26, 2025.

2. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy, with increase in size of some lymph nodes compared to CT on December 15, 2025.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.6 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Cardiomegaly.

6. Left adrenal adenoma, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

7. Hiatal hernia.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Shortness of breath, chronic shortness of breath; patient is on oxygen.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on December 15, 2025, August 26, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume mGy; total DLP mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 3.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Gravitational changes predominantly in lung bases. Linear atelectasis in the right lung bases.
Pulmonary nodules in series 4 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- A few clustered ground-glass nodules (>3), measuring from 2 mm to 4 mm, images 64-66.
- Solid nodule measuring up to 4 mm, image 70.
- Micronodule measuring up to 2 mm, image 70.
Left lower lobe:
- Micronodule measuring up to 2 mm, image 146.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly. Fluid in the pericardial recesses measures up to 1.7 cm in maximal thickness, qualitatively moderate.
Coronary calcification is present in the left main and left anterior descending coronary arteries, qualitatively moderate.
Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels:
Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Moderate calcification of the aortic arch. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Chronic wedge deformity of the T12 and L1 vertebral bodies without evidence of neural compression.

Upper abdomen:
Liver lesions are present - hypodense, homogeneous, density no greater than 12 HU, with well-defined margins, measuring 1.7 cm in diameter in segment 5, and 2.9 cm and 1.2 cm in segment 4a.
doc_impression:
1. Multiple subsolid nodules in the right upper lobe. Per Fleischner Society recommendations, follow-up CT in 3-6 months; if stable, consider CT at 2 and 4 years.

2. Single solid nodule and a few micronodules. Per Fleischner Society recommendations, no follow-up is required in low-risk patients; optional CT at 12 months in high-risk patients.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Fluid in the pericardial recesses measures up to 1.7 cm in maximal thickness, qualitatively moderate.

5. Aortic valve calcification.

6. A few liver lesions with benign features, likely cysts. No follow-up is required according to "Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee," 2017.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 4 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- A few clustered ground-glass nodules measure from 2 mm to 4 mm, images 64-66.
- Solid nodule measures up to 4 mm, image 70.
- Solid nodule measures up to 3 mm, image 68.
In the left lower lobe, a few micronodules measure up to 2 mm (image 146, 175).
Biapical thickening of the interlobular interstitium. Predominantly in the basal and subpleural areas, reticulation, ground-glass opacities and slight thickening of the interlobular interstitium. Minimal gravitational changes and linear atelectasis in lung bases. No honeycombing is identified. No pleural effusion. Mild wall thickening of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate, which is consistent with Cardiac CT on June 12, 2025. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of T11 - L1 vertebral bodies (118 HU) and wedge deformity of the T12 and L1 vertebral bodies up to 26% (grade 2 according to Genant classification) represent CT signs of demineralization. According to the 2020 bone density test, low bone mass was determined. 4 mm L1 retrolisthesis (grade I according to Meyerding classification). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen: Please see separately dictated abdomen CT, performed concurrently.
val_impression:
1. A few clustered ground-glass nodules in the right upper lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years. 
A few solid nodules up to 4 mm in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Bilateral interstitial changes with subpleural and basal predominance may represent interstitial lung abnormalities (Hatabu H, et al. Interstitial lung abnormalities detected incidentally on CT: a Position Paper from the Fleischner Society. Lancet Respir Med. 2020;8(7):726-737. doi:10.1016/S2213-2600(20)30168-5)

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate, which is consistent with Cardiac CT on June 12, 2025. 
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification. Thoracic aorta calcification.

5. CT signs of demineralization. L1 retrolisthesis, grade I according to Meyerding classification.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Abnormal weight loss, heart disease.

COMPARISON: Cardiac CT on June 12, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 17.59 mGy; total DLP 657.42 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Solid nodules identified in the lungs are as follows on series 5:
In the right lower lobe:
- Nodule measuring up to 1.0 × 0.7 cm (image 166) - previously 7 mm.
In the left upper lobe:
- Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 166) - unchanged.
A small number of calcifications are also present in the lungs, largest in the left lower lobe measuring up to 7 mm.
Linear atelectatic changes in the middle lobe, right paravertebral.
Reticular changes in the subpleural regions of the lungs, with areas of thickened interlobular interstitium in the lower basal regions on a background of gravitational changes.
No pleural effusion.
Trachea and major bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy.
Calcifications in 10L lymph nodes.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly.
No significant pericardial effusion.
Severe coronary artery calcification (extensive calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the right coronary artery and left circumflex artery, less pronounced in the left anterior descending artery).
Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels:
Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of large osteophytes.
Decreased density of vertebral bodies at T11-L1 to +124 HU.

Upper abdomen:
Low-density (-11 HU) masses in the adrenal glands: in the lateral limb on the right measuring up to 2.6 × 1.5 cm, in the body of the left adrenal gland measuring 2.2 × 1.2 cm (unchanged from the prior study and compared with CT dated February 03, 2025).
In the parenchyma of the mid left kidney, a fluid-density lesion measuring up to 1.0 × 0.9 cm with sharp margins (unchanged compared with CT dated February 03, 2025).
doc_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe - given increase in size compared with the prior study, per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, consider PET/CT or biopsy. Small solid nodule in the left upper lobe, unchanged. Interstitial lung changes (nonspecific interstitial pneumonia?) visually unchanged. Pulmonary calcifications.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

3. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at T11-L1.

5. Adrenal masses of benign phenotype: according to "Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2017", no additional evaluation or follow-up imaging is required.

6. Hypodense lesion in the parenchyma of the left kidney of fluid/cystic density: according to "Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018", in the absence of diseases and/or genetic syndromes predisposing to renal neoplasms, as well as in the absence of primary malignancies with a high likelihood of metastasizing to the kidneys (lung cancer, lymphoma, or melanoma), no further follow-up/additional evaluation is required.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid nodules identified in the lungs are as follows on series 5:
In the right lower lobe:
- Nodule measures up to 1.0 × 0.8 cm (image 166), On Chest CT on January 19, 2026, nodule measures 8 × 6 mm. On Chest CT on February 3, 2025, the nodule is not identified. 
In the left lower lobe:
- Calcified nodules measure 7 mm and 3 mm (image 223), unchanged.
Multiple, predominantly subpleural and basal, areas of ground-glass opacity with reticulations, thickening of the interlobular interstitium, traction bronchiectasis, and interval progression compared with Chest CT on February 3, 2025 due to the appearance of new areas of ground-glass. Mosaic attenuation is present in the lower lobes. No honeycombing is identified. In the right lower lobe, there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced fibrosis. No pleural effusion. Trachea and major bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcifications in the left pulmonary lymph nodes.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Severe coronary artery calcification, calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the right coronary artery, left circumflex artery, left anterior descending artery. 

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of large osteophytes. Degenerative changes of shoulder joints. Decreased density of vertebral bodies at T11-L1 to +124 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: Low-density (-11 HU) lesions in the adrenal glands (benign adenomas): in the lateral limb on the right measuring 1.5 cm, in the body of the left adrenal gland measuring 1.2 cm (unchanged).
In the parenchyma of the left kidney, a fluid-density lesion measuring up to 1.0 × 0.9 cm with sharp margins (unchanged), likely benign cyst.
val_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe, increased in size compared with Chest CT on January 19, 2026, likely of neoplastic etiology; consider PET/CT or biopsy.

2. Interstitial lung disease with a slight interval worsening compared with Chest CT on February 3, 2025 may correspond to fibrotic non-specific interstitial pneumonia or probable usual interstitial pneumonia according to Diagnostic HRCT criteria for usual interstitial pneumonia pattern - ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT (2018).

3. Calcified nodules in the left lower lobe and calcifications in the left pulmonary lymph nodes may represent healed granulomatous disease, require no follow-up.

4. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

5. Bilateral adrenal benign adenomas, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

6. Likely benign cyst of the left kidney. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: No need for further work-up.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Following nodules seen on lung cancer screening scan, current smoker, no current complaints.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on January 19, 2026, February 3, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 8.54 mGy; total DLP 316.29 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.
